title: Dear Jennifer Coolidge
description: Rewatching Jennifer Coolidge in Austenland feels like the emotional opposite of doomscrolling—a return to safe and comforting rather than a digital conveyor belt of brand-new bad news.
hook: Jennifer Coolidge's late-career glow isn't just winning—it's a radical act of refusing to perform doom for the algorithm.

title: Bolaño’s Heresy: On Distant Star
description: “Maybe this is my own dream or projection, but when I read Bolaño’s flat, minimal descriptions of nonexistent verse, the prose begins to vibrate.”
hook: Reading Bolaño's invented poems about nonexistent verse—the prose vibrates, and suddenly you understand how style becomes its own truth.

title: Awards Season and the Management of Cultural Power
description: What is being offered as recognition often operates as a way of organizing power, determining not only what is seen, but who is positioned to benefit from that visibility.
hook: Awards don't celebrate culture—they organize who gets to profit from it. Here's how power masquerades as recognition.

title: An open letter to Grammarly and other plagiarists, thieves and slop merchants
description:
To everyone at Grammarly,

I am writing a book right now, a really challenging endeavor that no doubt someone in Silicon Valley will think it’s fine to steal the day it’s published. I’ve been a professional writer for decades, even though the number of
hook: A working writer's fury at Silicon Valley stealing decades of craft. This isn't complaining—it's the fight for authorship itself.

title: “Whidbey” Confronts the Fantasy of Revenge
description:
The book begins on a boat to an island: a woman running from the man who abused her as a child, a chance meeting with a stranger who promises to kill him—and then, days later, the murder. Whidbey’s opening pages offer spare information and a heightened sense of threat, evoking the everyday experience of people […]

The post “Whidbey” Confronts the Fantasy of Revenge appeared first on
hook: A woman flees her childhood abuser, hires a stranger to kill him, and then the murder actually happens. Spare prose, unbearable tension.
